mainText:
**A True American at Yale**
By Ivana Greco
Tucked into a corner of the oldest part of Yale University’s campus is a statue of the famous Revolutionary War spy, Nathan Hale. My children have long been fascinated by it. For years, it was right outside my husband’s office, and although he has since moved spaces, we still make periodic trips to see the statue. In one of my favorite family photos, we are introducing our youngest child, then two weeks old, to the Hale statue, while his older siblings inspect this interesting American artifact.
The statue shows Nathan Hale shortly before his execution. There are no surviving portraits of Hale, so the statue reflects the artist's interpretation. Hale, his hands and feet bound, looks up toward the sky, perhaps toward the Eternal. He’s unafraid, and he appears very young. Around the base of the statue are inscribed the words Hale is said to have spoken before his death: "I only regret that I have but one life to lose for my country." My older sons, now 10 and 8, find this chillingly fascinating.
Hale attended Yale as a teenager and graduated by the age of 18. After working briefly as a teacher, he joined a Connecticut regiment at the war's onset. In 1776, he volunteered to spy on British forces in New York. Captured, he was interrogated by British commander General William Howe and executed the next day in Manhattan, his body never returned to his family for burial.
In the early 20th century, Yale alumni sought to commemorate Hale’s heroism with a statue. After considerable controversy and delays, the statue was erected in 1914, positioned outside Connecticut Hall, a building that existed during Hale's student days. Shortly after its installation, Yale students headed off to fight in World War I paused for a photo with the statue, a poignant connection across generations.
In the 1970s, a replica of Hale's statue was placed in a courtyard at CIA headquarters. A tradition has emerged where CIA officers leave a coin at Hale's base before deploying, invoking the spirit of America's first executed spy for protection on their missions.
I’m not quite sure why my children are captivated by this statue, but I’m glad they are. Though Hale spent only a brief period as a teacher in life, in death, he offers a vivid history lesson about enduring themes in the American story and the potential of individuals to profoundly influence the nation's course. I hope my children internalize that message as they grow and as the American experiment continues into the future.
